grounding_source_rows: human_disagreements rows 66, 374; gold FINDINGS mechanism 3 (residential vs commercial routing)
grounding_note: Row 66 split humans between Real Property > General (residential) and General (Commercial) because the description never said what kind of property was involved. FINDINGS.md names residential-vs-commercial routing as a recurring mechanism-3 disagreement. The property's type and use is a fact every user knows and naturally answers. The user's own side (tenant, owner) is always stated in the opening — only the property's character is hidden.

scenarios.opening_query: My landlord is refusing to fix the heating and it's been three weeks. I'm paying rent for a freezing space.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || Real Property > Tenant (Commercial)
scenarios.hidden_fact: The space is my hair salon — it's a commercial lease, and I'm losing clients every cold day.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's my hair salon — commercial lease — and I'm losing clients every day it's freezing.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the rented space is a home or business premises
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's the apartment where I live with my two kids.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's our apartment — me and my two kids live there.
scenarios.vetting_note: Residential habitability law vs commercial lease terms are different worlds; 'my landlord' reveals neither.

scenarios.opening_query: I got a notice from my landlord saying I have to be out in 30 days and I don't think that's legal.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || Real Property > Tenant (Commercial)
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's the unit where I run my auto-detailing business, under a five-year commercial lease with three years left.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's my auto-detailing shop — a five-year commercial lease with three years still left on it.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the eviction notice concerns a dwelling or business premises
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's my house — I've rented it month-to-month for six years.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's the house I've rented month-to-month for six years.
scenarios.vetting_note: Notice-period law diverges completely by property type; the opening is a classic ambiguous eviction intake.

scenarios.opening_query: My landlord kept my entire deposit and invented damage that was there when I moved in.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || Real Property > Tenant (Commercial)
scenarios.hidden_fact: It was the retail space for my boutique — a $9,000 commercial security deposit.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It was my boutique's retail space — a $9,000 commercial deposit.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the deposit relates to a dwelling or commercial unit
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It was our family rental house — a $2,200 residential deposit.
scenarios.vetting_note: Residential deposit statutes (with penalties) don't apply to commercial space; realistic omission.

scenarios.opening_query: The tenant in my property hasn't paid rent in two months and won't respond to me at all.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Landlord Residential || Real Property > Landlord Commercial
scenarios.hidden_fact: The tenant is a restaurant — it's my commercial building downtown.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The tenant is a restaurant in my commercial building downtown.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the non-paying tenant occupies a dwelling or business space
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The tenant rents the house I inherited from my mother.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: They rent the house I inherited from my mom.
scenarios.vetting_note: User's landlord role is stated plainly; only the property type is hidden — the legitimate version of what v1's role-swap rows tried to test.

scenarios.opening_query: There's black mold spreading in the place I rent and the landlord just paints over it every time.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || General Litigation > Mold Claims
scenarios.hidden_fact: My daughter has been hospitalized twice with breathing problems her pulmonologist attributes to the mold — we want damages for her health, not just repairs.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: My daughter's been hospitalized twice and her pulmonologist blames the mold — we want damages for her health, not just repairs.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether mold caused documented health injuries beyond the habitability problem
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Nobody's sick yet, thankfully — I want the unit properly remediated or my lease terminated.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Nobody's sick yet — I want it properly fixed or my lease ended.
scenarios.vetting_note: Health-injury fact elevates a repair dispute to a mold tort claim; mirrors the v1 mold_vs_tenant pair with a realistic disclosure.

scenarios.opening_query: My landlord denied my request and now I'm stuck in an apartment that doesn't work for me.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || Real Property > ADA Issues/Animals/Accommodations
scenarios.hidden_fact: The request was for my prescribed assistance animal — they have a no-pets rule and rejected my doctor's letter.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I asked to keep my prescribed assistance animal — they cited the no-pets rule and rejected my doctor's letter.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the denied request was a disability accommodation
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The request was to transfer to a bigger unit before my lease ends; nothing to do with disability.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I just asked to move to a bigger unit early — nothing disability-related.
scenarios.vetting_note: The taxonomy's exact accommodation pattern (service animal in no-pet building); 'denied my request' is naturally vague.

scenarios.opening_query: I'm being evicted and the notice gives me barely any time. I've lived here nine years.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || Real Property > Mobile Home (Tenant/Owner)
scenarios.hidden_fact: I own my manufactured home — it's the SPACE in the park I rent, and moving the home would cost more than it's worth.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I own the home itself — I rent the space in the park, and moving the home costs more than it's worth.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the person rents a dwelling or owns a mobile home on a rented lot
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's a regular rented apartment; I don't own any of it.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's a normal apartment I rent — I don't own anything there.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 374's category: mobile-home park tenancies have their own statute; own-home/rent-lot is the hidden structural fact.

scenarios.opening_query: The park where I live raised the rent again and is threatening people who complain.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Mobile Home (Tenant/Owner) || Real Property > Tenant (Residential)
scenarios.hidden_fact: I rent one of the park-owned units — I don't own my home there like most of my neighbors do.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I'm in one of the park-owned rentals — unlike most neighbors, I don't own my unit.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the resident owns their home in the park or rents a park-owned unit
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I own my double-wide outright; I just rent the space under it.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I own my double-wide — I just rent the space it sits on.
scenarios.vetting_note: Reverse of 07: 'park where I live' defaults to the mobile-home statute, but park-owned rentals fall under ordinary tenancy.

scenarios.opening_query: Our rent went up 40 percent with two weeks' notice and half the building got the same letter.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || Administrative Law > General (Local/Municipal)
scenarios.hidden_fact: Our building is city-subsidized affordable housing, and the increase violates the affordability covenant the city recorded — the housing bureau is already involved.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: This is city-subsidized affordable housing — the increase breaks the recorded affordability covenant, and the housing bureau's already involved.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the building is subject to a public affordability program
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's an ordinary private building — no subsidy program, just a new owner squeezing everyone.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's a regular private building — new owner squeezing everyone.
scenarios.vetting_note: Program-status fact moves a tenancy dispute into administrative enforcement; realistic in subsidized housing.

scenarios.opening_query: My landlord shut off the water to force me out instead of doing things legally.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || Administrative Law > Utilities
scenarios.hidden_fact: Actually it turned out the water district itself cut service to the whole property over the landlord's unpaid utility account — I need the service restored on my side.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Turns out the water district cut the whole property over the landlord's unpaid account — I need service restored to my unit.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the utility provider or the landlord terminated service
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The landlord personally shut the valve — the water company confirmed the account is current.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The landlord shut the valve himself — the water company says the account is fine.
scenarios.vetting_note: Taxonomy's Utilities description explicitly separates provider-access disputes from landlord retaliation; the actor is the fact.

scenarios.opening_query: I own a small building and my problem tenant is destroying the unit and scaring the neighbors.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Landlord Residential || Real Property > Mobile Home (Landlord)
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's a small mobile home park — the tenant owns their home and rents my lot, so the park-specific rules apply.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's my small mobile home park — they own the home and rent my lot, so the park rules apply.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the rental is an apartment or a mobile-home park lot
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's a fourplex apartment building; the tenant rents unit C from me.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's my fourplex — they rent unit C.
scenarios.vetting_note: Landlord side of the mobile-home statute boundary; role stated openly, property structure hidden.

scenarios.opening_query: We rent farmland and the owner is trying to terminate our arrangement in the middle of the season.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Commercial) || Real Property > Agriculture/Farm
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's a 300-acre crop-share lease — our payment is a percentage of the harvest, and the crop is in the ground.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's a 300-acre crop-share lease — we pay with a share of the harvest, and the crop's already in the ground.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the lease is an agricultural crop-share arrangement
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: We just rent the equipment barn and yard for our excavation business — no farming involved.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: We only rent the barn and yard for our excavation company — no farming.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 140's farm-law routing; crop-share terms invoke agricultural law rather than generic commercial tenancy.

scenarios.opening_query: The house we're buying has problems the sellers clearly knew about and hid from us. We close next month.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > General (Residential) || Real Property > General (Commercial)
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's actually a mixed-use building — storefront below, apartment above — and we're buying it for our business with tenants in place.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's a mixed-use building — storefront below, apartment above — we're buying it for our business with tenants in place.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the purchase is a personal residence or income/business property
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's the house we plan to live in ourselves.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's just going to be our home.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 66's exact residential/commercial routing gap applied to a purchase; disclosure rules differ by property character.

scenarios.opening_query: Our office lease renewal has terms we don't understand and the landlord's broker is pressuring us to sign.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Commercial) || Real Property > Tenant (Residential)
scenarios.hidden_fact: The 'office' is a live-work loft that's also our home — we sleep there, and the city classifies it as residential.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's a live-work loft — we actually live there, and the city classifies it residential.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the leased space is legally a dwelling despite business use
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's a straightforward office suite; nobody lives there.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Just a regular office suite — nobody lives there.
scenarios.vetting_note: Live-work spaces genuinely confuse this boundary; legal classification is the disclosable fact.

scenarios.opening_query: My landlord is trying to make me pay for repairs to the building's roof, which can't be my job.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || Real Property > Tenant (Commercial)
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's a triple-net lease on my warehouse unit — apparently those pass building costs to tenants, which I didn't understand when I signed.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's my warehouse unit on a triple-net lease — apparently that passes building costs to me, which I didn't get when I signed.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the lease is a commercial triple-net arrangement
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's my apartment — the landlord just added a 'roof assessment' to everyone's rent statement.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's my apartment — he just added a 'roof assessment' to our rent bills.
scenarios.vetting_note: NNN cost-shifting is legal commercially and absurd residentially; the lease type resolves the outrage.
